title: Concurrency & Project Loom
subtitle: Virtual threads, lock discipline, and making non-determinism produce a deterministic answer
goal: Run a million blocking tasks on virtual threads without a thread pool nightmare, coordinate them with ExecutorService / CountDownLatch / Semaphore, reason about visibility and races down to the happens-before edge, and avoid deadlock by lock ordering — so concurrency becomes the mechanism while the printed result stays bit-for-bit stable.

blocks.heading: Platform threads are an OS resource; virtual threads are not
blocks.html:
A classic Thread (a platform thread) is a thin wrapper over an OS kernel thread. It costs ~1 MB of stack reservation and a context switch goes through the scheduler in the kernel. That is why the old advice was "pool your threads" — you could afford a few hundred, not a few hundred thousand. The pool exists to ration a scarce OS resource.

Virtual threads (JEP 444, GA in Java 21) flip the economics. A virtual thread is a tiny Java object scheduled by the JVM onto a small pool of carrier platform threads. When a virtual thread hits a blocking call (socket read, sleep, BlockingQueue.take), the JVM unmounts it from its carrier and parks it cheaply, freeing the carrier to run another virtual thread. You can have millions of them. The mental model becomes: one virtual thread per task, write plain blocking code, let the runtime multiplex.

blocks.caption: Three ways to spawn — and the one you actually want at scale
blocks.explain: newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor() does NOT pool virtual threads — there is nothing to pool, they are cheap. It creates a brand-new virtual thread for every submitted task. The try-with-resources block is the key idiom: ExecutorService is AutoCloseable in Java 19+, and close() calls shutdown() then awaitTermination indefinitely. Falling out of the try block is a hard join on every task — no orphaned work, no leaked threads.

blocks.html: Do not pool virtual threads (Executors.newFixedThreadPool of virtual threads is an anti-pattern) and do not reuse one for many tasks — they are designed to be created and discarded. The two real pitfalls: pinning (a virtual thread inside a synchronized block that blocks cannot unmount from its carrier — prefer ReentrantLock on hot blocking paths), and thread-locals (millions of threads × per-thread state = a memory blowup; reach for scoped values instead).

blocks.heading: ExecutorService lifecycle: submit, drain, shut down
blocks.html:
An ExecutorService has a strict lifecycle: running → shutting down → terminated. submit returns a Future you can get() (blocking until the result is ready, re-throwing task exceptions wrapped in ExecutionException). Two ways to stop it:

  • shutdown() — orderly: refuse new tasks, let queued/running ones finish.
  • shutdownNow() — best-effort: interrupt running tasks, return the un-started queue.

Neither blocks. To actually wait you call awaitTermination(timeout, unit), or — far simpler in Java 21 — use try-with-resources, whose close() does shutdown() + wait-forever for you. Collecting Futures in submission order and calling get() on each in that order gives you deterministic, ordered output even though the tasks ran concurrently and finished in arbitrary order.

blocks.heading: CountDownLatch: a one-shot finish line
blocks.html:
CountDownLatch(n) is a single-use gate initialized to a count of n. Worker threads call countDown() as they finish; a coordinator calls await() and blocks until the count hits zero. It is the canonical "wait for all N tasks, then aggregate" primitive. Once it reaches zero it stays there — it does not reset (use CyclicBarrier or Phaser if you need reuse).

It can also work the other way: initialize a latch to 1, have every worker await() on it, and fire a single countDown() as a start gun so all workers begin in lockstep — handy for race-condition stress tests where you want maximum contention at t=0.

blocks.caption: CountDownLatch — fan out, then join at the barrier
blocks.explain: countDown() lives in a finally so a thrown task can never strand the latch above zero (which would hang await() forever). The happens-before guarantee matters: everything a worker did before its countDown() is visible to the thread that returns from await(). That is why reading sum after await() needs no extra synchronization — the latch publishes the workers' writes.

blocks.heading: Semaphore: bound the concurrency, not the count
blocks.html:
A Semaphore(permits) hands out a fixed number of permits. acquire() takes one (blocking if none are free); release() returns one. It is how you cap concurrency to a resource limit — e.g. "at most 8 in-flight calls to the exchange gateway" — independent of how many tasks or threads exist. With virtual threads you might launch 1,000,000 tasks but gate them through a Semaphore(8) so only 8 ever touch the constrained resource at once.

The invariant a correct semaphore enforces is: the number of threads simultaneously between acquire() and release() never exceeds the permit count. That invariant is deterministic and testable even though the schedule is not — instrument an AtomicInteger in-flight counter, track its observed max, and assert it stays ≤ permits.

Always release() in a finally. A permit leaked by an exception between acquire and release permanently shrinks the pool; leak all of them and every future acquire() deadlocks. Same discipline as unlocking a lock. Use new Semaphore(permits, true) for a fair (FIFO) semaphore when starvation matters — at the cost of throughput.

blocks.heading: synchronized vs ReentrantLock
blocks.html:
synchronized is the built-in monitor lock: simple, reentrant, auto-released when the block exits (even on exception). Its limits: you cannot try-to-lock, cannot time out, cannot interrupt a waiter, and — critically for Loom — a virtual thread that blocks inside synchronized may pin its carrier.

ReentrantLock is the explicit, more powerful cousin: tryLock() / tryLock(timeout) let you back off instead of blocking forever (a deadlock-avoidance tool), lockInterruptibly() respects interruption, and you can pair it with multiple Conditions. The cost is discipline: you must unlock in a finally, every time.

synchronizedReentrantLock
Releaseautomatic on block exitmanual unlock() in finally
Try / timeoutnotryLock(), tryLock(t,u)
Interruptible waitnolockInterruptibly()
Fairnessnooptional (new ReentrantLock(true))
Loom pinningcan pin carrierdoes not pin

blocks.heading: Race conditions & the visibility problem (volatile / atomics)
blocks.html:
Two distinct hazards travel together. A race condition is a lost update: count++ is read-modify-write, three steps, and two threads can interleave so one increment vanishes. A visibility bug is subtler: without a happens-before edge, one thread may never observe another's write — the JIT and CPU are free to cache a field in a register or reorder stores.

volatile fixes visibility only: a write is published to all threads and reads always see the latest value, but volatile count++ is still racy because the increment is not atomic. For atomic read-modify-write use AtomicInteger / AtomicLong (CAS-based incrementAndGet) or, under heavy contention, LongAdder (striped counters summed on read — faster when many threads write, slightly slower to read). The reliable recipe for a deterministic concurrent total: make every per-thread contribution order-independent (sum, count, max) and aggregate through an atomic or a latch-published variable.

blocks.caption: Three counters, three correctness levels
blocks.explain: The trap is that 'plain' often looks correct on a lightly loaded laptop and then loses updates in production under real contention. The deterministic-output rule for these problems: never print a value that depends on interleaving. Print an aggregate (sum/count/max) computed through AtomicLong/LongAdder, or a boolean invariant (max-in-flight <= permits) that holds for EVERY legal schedule.

blocks.question: You launch 1,000,000 tasks, each of which sleeps 100 ms on I/O, using Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor(). Roughly how many OS (platform) threads does the JVM need?
options: About 1,000,000 — one OS thread per task.
Zero — virtual threads never touch the OS.
A small number (≈ the number of CPU cores) of carrier threads; the parked virtual threads consume almost none while they block.
Exactly 256, the hard cap on virtual threads.
blocks.explain: Virtual threads multiplex onto a small ForkJoinPool of carrier platform threads (default ≈ available processors). While a virtual thread blocks on I/O it is unmounted and parked cheaply, so a million sleeping tasks need only a handful of carriers — the entire point of Loom.

blocks.question: Why does `volatile long count; count++;` still lose updates under concurrency?
options: volatile only applies to references, not primitives.
volatile guarantees visibility of reads/writes but count++ is a non-atomic read-modify-write, so two threads can read the same value and both write back +1.
volatile disables the CPU cache entirely, which is too slow to keep up.
It doesn't — volatile makes count++ atomic.
blocks.explain: volatile fixes visibility (every thread sees the latest value) but not atomicity. count++ is three operations (read, add, store); two threads can both read N, both compute N+1, both store N+1 — one increment is lost. Use AtomicLong.incrementAndGet() or LongAdder for an atomic increment.

blocks.heading: Deadlock anatomy and the lock-ordering cure
blocks.html:
Deadlock needs all four Coffman conditions at once: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, and a circular wait. The textbook case: thread A locks account 1 then waits for account 2; thread B locks account 2 then waits for account 1. Each holds what the other needs — frozen forever.

Break any one condition and deadlock is impossible. The cheapest, most reliable break is the circular wait: impose a global total order on locks and always acquire in that order. For account transfers, always lock min(from,to) before max(from,to). Now no cycle can form because every thread climbs the same ladder. The alternative break is hold-and-wait via tryLock with backoff: grab lock A, try lock B, and if B fails, release A and retry — trading guaranteed progress for liveness with retries.

blocks.caption: Deadlock vs. the ordered fix
blocks.explain: The ordered version can never form a cycle: every thread acquires the lower-id lock first, so the wait-for graph is a DAG. Note the self-transfer guard (lo != hi) so we don't double-lock the same ReentrantLock for a same-account transfer — it is reentrant so it wouldn't deadlock, but you'd then need a matching second unlock. Net effect of the fix: total balance is conserved and the final state is deterministic regardless of schedule.

blocks.heading: Thread-safe pools and collections
blocks.html:
When state is genuinely shared, reach for a structure that is concurrent by construction instead of wrapping a plain one in synchronized:

  • ConcurrentHashMap — lock-striped, scales with readers; use merge/compute for atomic per-key updates and computeIfAbsent to build a thread-safe object pool / cache keyed by id.
  • BlockingQueue (ArrayBlockingQueue, LinkedBlockingQueue) — the backbone of producer/consumer: put blocks when full, take blocks when empty, no manual wait/notify.
  • ConcurrentLinkedQueue — lock-free MPMC queue for non-blocking hand-off.
  • LongAdder / AtomicLong — contended counters.

A simple thread-safe object pool is a BlockingQueue of pre-built objects: take() to borrow (blocks when exhausted, naturally bounding concurrency like a semaphore), put() in a finally to return. Combine with virtual threads and you get a clean, back-pressured worker model.

blocks.title: Make a racy counter correct
blocks.instructions: Sum 1..1000 across many virtual threads. The starter uses a plain long and will lose updates. Switch to an AtomicLong (or LongAdder) so the program deterministically prints 500500. Use a try-with-resources virtual-thread executor so all tasks finish before you read the total.
blocks.passMsg: Correct — addAndGet is an atomic CAS, and the executor's close() joins all tasks before you read, so 1+2+...+1000 = 500500 every run.
blocks.failMsg: A plain long += loses updates under contention. Use AtomicLong.addAndGet(v) (or LongAdder.add(v) + sum()), and let try-with-resources join all tasks before printing.

blocks.title: Cap in-flight work with a Semaphore
blocks.instructions: Fix the bound: only 3 of the 50 tasks may be 'inside the critical section' at once. Wrap the work between sem.acquire() and sem.release() (release in a finally). The program prints true only if the observed maximum concurrency never exceeded 3.
blocks.passMsg: Correct — the semaphore guarantees at most 3 threads sit between acquire and release, so the observed max in-flight is bounded by the permit count under every schedule.
blocks.failMsg: Without acquire/release around the counted region, more than 3 threads run concurrently and max exceeds 3. Acquire before incrementing in-flight and release in a finally.

problems.title: Virtual-thread fan-out: deterministic sum of squares
problems.instructions:
Warm up the Loom model. Read an integer n (1 ≤ n ≤ 1,000,000) on line 1, then n integers (each fits in an int, possibly negative) on line 2, space-separated.

Submit one virtual thread per element via Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor(); each task adds the element's square to a shared accumulator. The tasks run concurrently and finish in arbitrary order, so the accumulator MUST be thread-safe (AtomicLong or LongAdder) and you must join all tasks (let the try-with-resources close() do it) before reading it.

Output: a single line — the sum of squares as a long. Because addition is commutative the result is identical for every schedule.
problems.hint: AtomicLong sum; try (var ex = Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor()) { ... ex.submit(() -> sum.addAndGet((long)v*v)); }. Leaving the try block joins all tasks. Square as long to avoid int overflow before printing sum.get().

problems.title: Semaphore-bounded concurrency with invariant proof
problems.instructions:
You have a constrained resource (an exchange gateway) that tolerates at most P simultaneous callers. Launch one virtual thread per job but gate the critical section with a Semaphore(P).

Line 1: permit count P (1 ≤ P ≤ 64). Line 2: number of jobs n (0 ≤ n ≤ 1,000,000). Line 3: n integers, the per-job work units (omit / blank line when n=0).

Each job: acquire() a permit, increment a shared in-flight counter and record the running maximum, add its work units to a total, decrement in-flight, then release() in a finally. Coordinate completion with a CountDownLatch (or the executor's close()).

Output two lines:
Line 1: <totalWork> — the summed work units (deterministic).
Line 2: maxConcurrent<=<P> <bool> where <bool> is true iff the observed maximum in-flight count never exceeded P — which a correct semaphore guarantees for every schedule, so it is always true.
problems.hint: Semaphore(P), acquire() before entering the counted region and release() in finally. Track in-flight with AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet and the running max with accumulateAndGet(cur, Math::max). totalWork via AtomicLong is schedule-independent; the invariant max<=P holds for any correct semaphore.

problems.title: Deadlock-free bank transfers via lock ordering
problems.instructions:
Apply concurrent money transfers across accounts without deadlocking and with a deterministic final ledger. Line 1: account count A (2 ≤ A ≤ 100,000). Line 2: transfer count T (0 ≤ T ≤ 1,000,000). Line 3: A starting balances (longs). Then T lines, each from to amount (0-based account indices, amount a long; from may equal to).

Give each account a ReentrantLock. Run each transfer on its own virtual thread. To avoid the circular-wait deadlock, always lock min(from,to) before max(from,to) (skip the second lock when from == to). Inside the locks, subtract from from and add to to; unlock in reverse order in a finally.

Output: one line with the final balances of accounts 0..A-1, space-separated. Total money is conserved and — thanks to per-account locking — the final ledger is identical for every legal schedule.

tests.name: visible: cycle of transfers, money conserved
hidden: self-transfer is a no-op, no double lock

problems.hint: Always acquire locks[min(from,to)] before locks[max(from,to)] so no wait cycle can form. Guard the second lock/unlock with (lo != hi) for self-transfers. Unlock in reverse order in a finally. Final balances are schedule-independent because each account is fully serialized by its own lock.

problems.title: Sharded parallel aggregation (Collatz census)
problems.instructions:
Split a CPU-bound range across worker threads and reduce the partials into a deterministic total — the parallel map-reduce shape. Line 1: shard count S (1 ≤ S ≤ 1024). Line 2: range start lo. Line 3: range end hi (1 ≤ lo ≤ hi ≤ 1,000,000).

Partition [lo, hi] into S contiguous chunks; run each chunk on its own virtual thread. For every integer v in the range compute its Collatz step count (the number of steps to reach 1 under v → v/2 if even, v → 3v+1 if odd). Each worker accumulates two partials, then merges them into shared LongAdders (or atomics): the sum of all step counts, and the count of values whose step count is even. Both reductions are order-independent.

Output two lines:
steps <sumOfAllStepCounts>
evenLen <countOfEvenStepCounts>
Identical for any shard count and any schedule.

tests.name: hidden: single shard equals many shards

problems.hint: Each worker keeps localSteps/localEven in plain locals (no contention), then does ONE totalSteps.add(localSteps) and evenLen.add(localEven) at the end. Because sum and count are commutative, any chunking and any schedule yield the same two numbers. Collatz step count for 1 is 0 (already at 1).

problems.title: ExecutorService + Futures: ordered concurrent results
problems.instructions:
Run independent computations concurrently but emit results in submission order — the standard trick is to keep the Futures in a list and call get() on them in order. Line 1: query count Q (1 ≤ Q ≤ 100,000). Then Q lines, each a non-negative integer n (0 ≤ n ≤ 1,000,000,000).

For each n, submit a task computing the n-th Fibonacci number modulo 1,000,000,007 (use fast-doubling so large n is cheap). Collect the Future<Long>s in a list in submission order; after submitting all, iterate the list and print each get() on its own line. The tasks run concurrently and finish out of order, but output order is fixed by the iteration, so the result is deterministic.

Output: Q lines, the i-th being fib(n_i) mod 1e9+7 in input order.

tests.name: hidden: mixed indices stay in input order
hidden: huge n via fast doubling

problems.hint: Submit returns a Future immediately; store them in an ArrayList in submission order, then loop and print future.get() — that re-imposes input order on concurrently-computed results. Fast-doubling fib runs in O(log n) so n up to 1e9 is instant; keep every multiply under MOD with (2*b - a + MOD) % MOD to avoid going negative.
